1: waṯṯēṣēʾ dînāh bat-lēʾāh ʾăšer yālaḏāh lĕyaʿăqōḇ liroot bĕḇĕnôt hāʾāreṣ — "Dinah went out to see (lirʾōt) the daughters of the land." The verb yāṣāʾ (to go out) combined with rāʾāh (to see) describes normal social mobility for a young woman — visiting female peers was customary. The narrator's use of rāʾāh (to see) is ironic: the following verse opens with Shechem "seeing" (wayyarʾ) her. The two seeings — Dinah's social seeing and Shechem's predatory seeing — are placed in immediate narrative juxtaposition. Dinah is identified by her maternal lineage (daughter of Leah) rather than the expected paternal lineage, perhaps emphasizing her position within the household hierarchy.
2: wayyarʾ ʾōtāh šĕḵem ben-ḥămôr haḥiwwî nĕśîʾ hāʾāreṣ wayyiqqaḥ ʾōtāh wayyiškaḇ ʾōtāh wayĕʿannehā — "When Shechem saw her, he took her and lay with her and violated her." Four verbs — rāʾāh (saw), lāqaḥ (took), šāḵaḇ (lay with), ʿinnāh — form a rapid sequence of predatory action. The Piel of ʿānāh (H6031) — to humble, afflict, violate by force — is the technical term for sexual violence in the legal and narrative texts (2 Samuel 13:12,14,22,32 for Amnon's rape of Tamar; Deuteronomy 22:24,29). The word is not "seduced" or "took by persuasion" — the narrative's moral vocabulary is explicit. Shechem's title nĕśîʾ hāʾāreṣ (prince/ruler of the land) is a narrative detail: the one who commits the act is the one with the most social power to do it with impunity.
3: waṯṯiḏbaq napšô bĕḏînāh bat-yaʿăqōḇ wayyeʾĕhaḇ ʾet-hannaʿărāh wayyĕḏabbēr ʿal-lēḇ hannaʿărāh — "His soul clung to Dinah and he loved the young woman and spoke tenderly to her." Three phrases of attachment follow the rape: dāḇaq (H1692, to cling, cleave — the marriage-bonding verb of Genesis 2:24: "a man shall cleave to his wife"), ʾāhaḇ (loved), and dāḇar ʿal-lēḇ (spoke to the heart — the idiom for persuasive, intimate speech in Judges 19:3 [Levite to his concubine]; Isaiah 40:2 [God comforting Jerusalem]). The irony is structural: the language of covenantal love and bonding in Genesis 2:24 is applied here to a relationship initiated by violence. The post-hoc attachment does not undo the act; the narrative refuses to allow the subsequent "love" to reframe the rape as seduction.
4: wayyōʾmer šĕḵem ʾel-ḥămôr ʾāḇîw lēʾmōr qah-lî ʾet-hayyaldāh hazzōʾt lĕʾiššāh — "Shechem said to his father Hamor, 'Get me this girl as my wife.'" The demand form — qah-lî (take for me, imperative of lāqaḥ, the same verb used for his taking of Dinah in v2) — treats the woman as an object of acquisition. The word yaldāh (girl, young woman, H3207) rather than ʾiššāh (woman) in the description but ʾiššāh in the desired outcome — "as my wife" — shows Shechem casting the relationship in legitimate marital terms without acknowledging what he has done. The transaction is directed to the father (Hamor), following ANE betrothal custom where the father negotiates.
5: wĕyaʿăqōḇ šāmaʿ kî ṭimmēʾ ʾet-dînāh bittô ûḇānāyw hāyû ʾet-miqnēhû baśśāḏeh wĕheḥĕrîš yaʿăqōḇ ʿaḏ-bōʾām — "Jacob heard that his daughter Dinah had been defiled (ṭimmēʾ), but he kept silent (heḥĕrîš) until his sons came home." Ṭimmēʾ (Piel of ṭāmēʾ, H2930, to defile, make impure) encompasses both moral and ritual impurity — the same root used for the holiness code's pollution categories. Heḥĕrîš (Hiphil of ḥārash, H2790, to be silent, to make no sound) — Jacob's silence contrasts sharply with his sons' fury in v7. The brothers' anger will be described as ḥārāh (v7); Jacob's words in v30 reveal his silence was prudential rather than moral — he was calculating the risk to himself.
6: wayyēṣēʾ ḥămôr ʾăḇî-šĕḵem ʾel-yaʿăqōḇ lĕḏabbēr ʾittô — "Hamor went out to Jacob to speak with him." The verbs of movement (yāṣāʾ, went out) and speech (dāḇar, to speak) frame the formal diplomatic encounter. Hamor approaches Jacob directly, bypassing the convention of allowing the woman's family to initiate. This puts Jacob in a reactive rather than active position — he must listen to Hamor's proposal before the brothers arrive. The narrator's pace slows at this point, anticipating the brothers' arrival and their deceptive counter-proposal.
7: wĕhēmmāh ḥārû wayyihar ʾănaššîm mĕʾōḏ kî nĕḇālāh ʿāśāh bĕyiśrāʾēl liškōḇ ʾet-bat-yaʿăqōḇ wĕḵēn lōʾ yēʿāśeh — "They were furious, for Shechem had committed an outrage (nĕḇālāh) in Israel — a thing that ought not to be done." Nĕḇālāh (H5039, disgrace, shameful folly, outrage) is a technical legal term for the most serious violations of social and moral order — used for Achan's sin (Joshua 7:15), Amnon's rape of Tamar (2 Samuel 13:12: "don't do this nĕḇālāh"), and sexual disorder in Judges 19:23-24. The accompanying formula wĕḵēn lōʾ yēʿāśeh (and so it ought not to be done) is a legal prohibition-formula signaling that the act constitutes a category violation. The phrase "in Israel" (bĕyiśrāʾēl) anticipates the people of Israel before the nation exists — projecting the tribal identity backward onto Jacob's household.
8: wayyĕḏabbēr ḥămôr ʾittām lēʾmōr šĕḵem bĕnî ḥāšĕqāh napšô bĕḇitteḵem — "My son Shechem — his soul longs for your daughter." Ḥāšaq (H2836, to long for, to be strongly attached to) is a rare verb expressing intense desire or bonding — used for YHWH's attachment to Israel in Deuteronomy 7:7 ("YHWH set his love on you") and Genesis 10:15 ("YHWH delighted in your fathers"). The verb frames Shechem's desire in the language of covenant-election love, which makes Hamor's proposal sound more compelling while obscuring the assault that initiated it. The diplomatic presentation of Shechem's desire as ḥāšaq-love is a rhetorical strategy.
9: wĕhitḥattĕnû ʾōtānû bĕnōtêḵem tittĕnû-lānû wĕʾet-bĕnōtênû tiqqĕḥû lāḵem — "Intermarry with us — give us your daughters, take our daughters for yourselves." Hithḥatthēn (Hithpael of ḥātan, H2859, to become related by marriage) — the verb for entering the covenant of marital alliance. In Deuteronomy 7:3 this specific verb is prohibited for Israel with Canaanite peoples: lōʾ tithḥatthēn bām (you shall not intermarry with them). Hamor's proposal is precisely what the Deuteronomic law will forbid — the assimilation of the covenant people into Canaanite social structure through intermarriage. The brothers' response is a refusal to assimilate, though their means of refusal (deception and massacre) is not commended.
10: wĕʾittānû tēšēḇû wĕhāʾāreṣ tihyeh lipnêḵem šĕḇû wĕsĕḥārûhā wĕhēʾāḥăzû ḇāh — "Dwell with us; the land is before you — live in it, trade in it, acquire property in it." Three verbs — šāḇaṯ (dwell), sāḥar (H5503, to go about trading, to trade/traffic), ʾāḥaz (H270, to seize, hold, acquire) — describe the economic integration being offered. Sāḥar (the merchant-verb) is from the same root as Issachar and the commercial vocabulary of Genesis 37-45. Hamor's offer is genuine assimilation — not merely marital but economic and territorial. The phrase "the land is before you" (hāʾāreṣ tihyeh lipnêḵem) echoes the formulation YHWH will use for the promised land.
11: wayyōʾmer šĕḵem ʾel-ʾāḇîhā wĕʾel-ʾaḥêhā ʾemṣāʾ-ḥēn bĕʿênêḵem wĕʾăšer tōʾmĕrû ʾēlāy ʾettēn — "Let me find favor (ḥēn) in your eyes, and I will give whatever you say." Shechem appropriates the covenantal favor-language Jacob used before Esau (Genesis 32:5; Genesis 33:8,10,15) and that Jacob had used before Laban (Genesis 30:27). Māṣāʾ ḥēn bĕʿênê (to find grace/favor in the eyes of) is the formula of covenantal petition — seeking the acceptance of a greater party by a lesser. Shechem's adoption of this formula positions him as the suppliant; the irony is that he violated the very person in whose family he is now seeking inclusion.
12: harḇû ʿālay mĕʾōḏ môhar ûmattān wĕʾettĕnāh kaʾăšer tōʾmĕrû ʾēlāy ûtĕnû-lî ʾet-hannaʿărāh lĕʾiššāh — "Make the bride-price and gift very high — I will pay whatever you say." Môhar (H4119, bride-price) — the payment made by the groom's family to the bride's family, establishing the legal transfer of the woman into marriage. Mattān (H4976, gift) — an additional gift, perhaps to the bride herself. Shechem's willingness to pay any price reveals both his desire and his family's wealth; it also signals that the subsequent circumcision-demand is within reach for a wealthy household. The commercial vocabulary of marriage negotiation is explicit here — marriage as legal-economic transaction.
13: wayyaʿănû ḇĕnê-yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-šĕḵem wĕʾet-ḥămôr ʾāḇîw bĕmirmāh wayyĕḏabbĕrû ʾăšer ṭimmēʾ ʾēt dînāh ʾăḥōtām — "Jacob's sons answered Shechem and his father Hamor with deceit (bĕmirmāh)." Mirmāh (H4820, deceit, treachery) — the same word used for Jacob's own deceit of Isaac in Genesis 27:35: "your brother came bĕmirmāh and took your blessing." The narrator names the deception explicitly before describing the proposal, signaling that what follows is to be read as manipulation rather than covenant negotiation. The pattern of familial deception — Isaac deceived by Jacob, Jacob deceived by Laban, Shechem/Hamor deceived by Jacob's sons — runs through Genesis as a recurring moral motif without divine endorsement.
14: lōʾ nûḵal laʿăśōt haddāḇār hazzeh lātēt ʾet-ʾăḥōtênû lĕʾîš ʾăšer-lô ʿorlāh kî-ḥerpāh hîʾ lānû — "We cannot give our sister to a man who has a foreskin — that would be a disgrace (ḥerpāh) to us." Ḥerpāh (H2781, reproach, disgrace, public shame) — the same word Rachel used for barrenness (Genesis 30:23: "God has taken away my reproach"). Circumcision as the boundary marker of covenant membership is deployed here as a deceptive demand — the brothers use the covenant sign as a weapon rather than as a genuine invitation to covenant inclusion. The irony is mordant: the sign of Abraham's covenant becomes the mechanism of its desecration.
15: bĕzōʾt niʾôt lāḵem ʾim tihyû ḵāmōnû lĕhimmōl lāḵem kol-zāḵār — "On this one condition we will agree with you — if you become like us by circumcising all your males." The condition is stated in terms of equivalence: lĕhimmōl lāḵem (to circumcise yourselves) and the goal "if you become like us" (ḵāmōnû). The brothers frame circumcision as the entry condition for covenant-community membership — which is its genuine theological meaning — but their purpose is not inclusion. The Niphal niʾôt (we will consent) from ʾāwāh (to desire, consent) marks their agreement as conditional and transactional.
16: wĕnāḇōʾ ʾittĕḵem wĕhāyînû lĕʿam ʾeḥāḏ — "We will come among you and become one people (lĕʿam ʾeḥāḏ)." The phrase ʿam ʾeḥāḏ (one people) — the goal of political assimilation. The covenant formula "one people" is the positive outcome of the brothers' deceptive negotiation. The narrator's reader, who has been told in v13 that this is mirmāh, knows this outcome will not be realized. The language of becoming "one people" will return in Ezra-Nehemiah's crisis of intermarriage and in the NT (Ephesians 2:14-15) as the actual theological reality in Christ.
17: wĕʾim-lōʾ tišmĕʿû ʾēlênû lāqaḥtî ʾet-bittênû wĕhālaḵnû — "If you do not listen to us, we will take our sister and go." The ultimatum employs lāqaḥ (take) — the same verb used for Shechem's taking of Dinah in v2. The brothers' threat mirrors the structure of Shechem's act: he took what was not given; they will take back what was taken. The rhetorical symmetry may be intentional — the narrative is structured around takings and counter-takings.
18: wayyîṭĕḇû diḇrêhem bĕʿênê ḥămôr ûḇĕʿênê šĕḵem ben-ḥămôr — "Their proposal seemed good in the eyes of Hamor and Shechem." The idiom yāṭaḇ bĕʿênê (to be good in the eyes of) is the standard formula for approval or assessment. Hamor and Shechem evaluate the proposal by the pleasure it brings Shechem and the commercial advantage it offers the city (v23: "will not their livestock become ours?"). The mutual evaluations — the brothers calculating deception (v13), the Shechemites calculating advantage (v23) — meet at the circumcision-covenant as the space where both groups believe they are getting what they want.
19: wĕlōʾ-ʾiḥar hannaʿar laʿăśôt haddāḇār kî ḥāpēṣ bĕḇat-yaʿăqōḇ wĕhûʾ niḵbāḏ mikkōl bêt ʾāḇîw — "The young man wasted no time doing it, for he delighted in Jacob's daughter; and he was the most honored (niḵbāḏ) in all his father's household." Niḵbāḏ (Niphal of kāḇaḏ, H3513, to be heavy/weighty/honored) — the most honored member of Hamor's household. The combination of social honor and emotional desire (ḥāpēṣ, H2654, delighted, took pleasure in) makes Shechem's compliance immediate. His haste and honor are both turned against him by the deception.
20: wayyāḇōʾ ḥămôr ûšĕḵem bĕnô ʾel-šaʿar ʿîrām wayyĕḏabbĕrû ʾel-ʾanĕšê ʿîrām lēʾmōr — "Hamor and his son Shechem came to the gate of their city and spoke to the men of their city." The city gate (šaʿar, H8179) was the civic assembly space and judicial forum in ANE cities — where elders sat, contracts were ratified, and legal cases adjudicated (cf. Ruth 4:1-12; Amos 5:12,15; Proverbs 31:23). Addressing the men at the gate is the formal civic mechanism for ratifying a community decision. Hamor and Shechem present the circumcision-covenant to the whole city.
21: hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh šĕlēmîm hēm ʾittānû wĕyēšĕḇû ḇāʾāreṣ wĕyisĕḥărû ḇāh wĕhāʾāreṣ hinněh raḥăḇat-yāḏayim lipnêhem — "These men are peaceable toward us. Let them live in the land and trade in it — the land is wide enough for them." Šĕlēmîm (peaceful, from šālôm) — Hamor describes Jacob's family as peaceable/covenant-friendly; the word ironically shares the root with the šālēm of 33:18 where Jacob arrived "safely." The commercial appeal (yisĕḥărû, to trade, same root as merchant) frames the proposal to the city in economic terms. The men of the city are convinced by the prospect of trade and land-sharing, not by love of Shechem.
22: ʾaḵ-bĕzōʾt yēʾōtû lānû hāʾănāšîm lāšeḇet ʾittānû lihyôt lĕʿam ʾeḥāḏ bĕhimmôl lānû kol-zāḵār — "Only on this condition will the men consent to live with us — to become one people, if every male among us is circumcised." The condition is presented as merely the procedural requirement for the commercial union. The city's acceptance of circumcision as a reasonable price for economic integration underscores that the sign of Abraham's covenant has been fully secularized in this negotiation — it is treated as a cultural adaptation for commercial advantage rather than as a covenant mark with theological content.
23: miqnēhem wĕqinyānām wĕkol-bĕhemtām halōʾ lānû hēm ʾaḵ nēʾôtāh lāhem wĕyēšĕḇû ʾittānû — "Won't their livestock, property, and all their animals become ours?" Hamor's commercial appeal to the city's men reveals the transaction's true logic: circumcision is the price for absorbing a wealthy pastoral clan. The rhetorical question (halōʾ) invites the men to calculate the economic benefit. This is the narrator's subtle indictment: both parties are transacting in bad faith — the brothers intending massacre, the Shechemites intending economic absorption.
24: wayyišmĕʿû ʾel-ḥămôr wĕʾel-šĕḵem bĕnô kol-yōṣĕʾê šaʿar ʿîrô wayyimmōlû kol-zāḵār — "All who went out of the city gate agreed with Hamor and Shechem, and every male was circumcised." The legal assembly (gate = civic forum) ratifies the decision unanimously. The mass circumcision of the city's males is presented as a collective civic act — one that will render every man in the city incapacitated on the third day (v25). The irony that the covenant sign of Israel becomes the mechanism of a city's destruction runs through the entire chapter.
25: wayhî bayyôm haššĕlîšî bihyôtām kōʾăḇîm wayyiqqĕḥû šĕnê ḇĕnê-yaʿăqōḇ šimʿôn wĕlēwî ʾăḥê dînāh ʾîš ḥarḇô wayyāḇōʾû ʿal-hāʿîr beṭaḥ — "On the third day, when they were in pain (kōʾăḇîm), two of Jacob's sons, Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers, took their swords and attacked the unsuspecting city." Bayyôm haššĕlîšî (on the third day) — the third day is a significant temporal marker throughout the OT: the day of accountability, of revelation, of reversal (cf. Genesis 22:4 — Abraham's third-day arrival at Moriah; Hosea 6:2 — "on the third day he will raise us up"). The pain (kōʾeḇ, H3510, anguish/pain) renders the entire male population defenseless. Beṭaḥ (H983, in security/unsuspecting) — the same word used for Israel's covenant-security promise when obedient (Leviticus 26:5; Deuteronomy 33:28); here it marks the city's vulnerability as the very security they were promised.
26: wĕʾet-ḥămôr wĕʾet-šĕḵem bĕnô hārĕgû lĕpî-ḥāreḇ wayyiqqĕḥû ʾet-dînāh mibbêt šĕḵem wayyēṣēʾû — "They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with the sword and took Dinah from Shechem's house and left." The inclusion of wayyiqqĕḥû ʾet-dînāh (they took Dinah) — using the same verb lāqaḥ as Shechem's taking in v2 — reframes the rescue as a counter-taking. The narrative throughout Genesis 34 is structured around lāqaḥ (take): Shechem took Dinah (v2), Jacob's sons took their swords (v25), they took Dinah back (v26), they took the city's livestock (v28). The tit-for-tat structure of taking frames the entire episode.
27: ḇĕnê yaʿăqōḇ bāʾû ʿal-haḥălālîm wayyāḇōzzû hāʿîr ʾăšer ṭimmĕʾû ʾăḥôtām — "The sons of Jacob came upon the dead and plundered the city that had defiled their sister." The narrator calls them ḇĕnê yaʿăqōḇ (sons of Jacob) rather than ḇĕnê yiśrāʾēl (sons of Israel) — the pre-covenant name, perhaps marking the action as falling short of covenant identity. ḇāzaz (H962, to plunder, spoil) — the verb of warfare plunder. The stated justification — "the city that defiled their sister" — applies the guilt of Shechem to the entire city, a collective attribution the text leaves morally unresolved.
28: ʾet-ṣōʾnām wĕʾet-bĕqārām wĕʾet-ḥămōrêhem wĕʾēt ʾăšer-bāʿîr wĕʾet-ʾăšer baśśāḏeh lāqāḥû — "Their flocks and herds and donkeys — whatever was in the city and in the field — they took." The comprehensive plunder — ṣōʾn (flocks), bāqār (cattle), ḥămōr (donkeys) — is the full pastoral inventory. The inventory formula (livestock + field) is standard for plunder descriptions in the OT warfare tradition (cf. Numbers 31:9; Joshua 8:27). The magnitude of the plunder transforms a personal honor-crime into a war of conquest with its full economic consequences.
29: wĕʾet-kol-ḥêlām wĕʾet-kol-ṭappām wĕʾet-nĕšêhem šāḇû wayyāḇōzzû wĕʾēt kol-ʾăšer babbāyit — "All their wealth and all their children and their women they took captive, and they plundered everything in the houses." The taking of women and children as captives (šāḇā, H7617, to take captive) is a war practice the Deuteronomic law will regulate (Deuteronomy 20:14; Genesis 21:10-14). Jacob's explicit protest in v30 focuses on practical danger to his household, not on the moral dimensions of enslaving the survivors.
30: wayyōʾmer yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-šimʿôn wĕʾel-lēwî ʿăḵarttem ʾōtî lĕhabbîʾšenî bĕyōšēḇ hāʾāreṣ baKĕnaʿănî ûḇapperizzî — "Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, 'You have brought trouble on me (ʿăḵarttem) by making me odious (lehabbîšenî)…'" ʿāḵar (H5916, to trouble, to roil/disturb) — the same root as the name Achan (Joshua 7:1) and the Valley of Achor; it means to trouble someone by stirring up difficulty that brings consequences. Bahʾ (Hiphil of bāʾaš, H887, to stink/make odious) — Jacob's concern is his reputation and the safety of his household among the Canaanites, not the justice or injustice of the act. The contrast between Jacob's self-focused prudential concern (vv30) and the brothers' honor-defense (v31) is the moral fulcrum of the chapter.
31: wayyōʾmĕrû hăḵizonāh yaʿăśeh ʾet-ʾăḥōtênû — "Shall he treat our sister like a prostitute (zônāh)?" Zônāh (H2181, harlot, prostitute — Qal participle of zānāh, to commit sexual immorality) — the brothers' final word reframes Dinah's defilement through the honor-shame category of sexual availability to multiple men without covenant. In their logic, Shechem's act without marriage-covenant treated Dinah as a zônāh — a woman available outside covenant protection. The question-form of their closing statement is rhetorically unanswerable; Jacob's objection is not replied to. The narrative ends without resolving the competing claims — Jacob's safety, the brothers' honor, Dinah's silence.
